channel.title: AISI Blog
channel.description: Blog posts from the AI Security Institute

item.title: Finding Cloud Misconfigurations with Frontier AI: A Case Study
item.description: A cybersecurity exercise from AISI’s engineering team, using frontier models to test our research platform for misconfigurations.

item.title: More compute, more capability: Why AI agent evaluations need to account for test-time compute
item.description: Standard evaluations cap how much compute AI agents can use. We show that raising those caps changes measured capability, the difficulty of tasks agents can solve, and how fast the frontier appears to move.

item.title: Releasing AISI’s Engineering Playbook
item.description: Building on the momentum of the Inspect toolkit, we’re open-sourcing parts of the research stack behind AISI's evaluations.

item.title: RealityTest: Do AI systems disclose their identity when asked?
item.description: A new benchmark grounded in how real users actually probe AI identity during interactions – covering five languages, across text and speech.

item.title: Deepening our partnership with the Australian AI Safety Institute
item.description: An agreement between Institutes to collaborate on best practices in AI evaluation, and share research findings.

item.title: Will it become harder to oversee AI systems?
item.description: Our new report examines today’s AI oversight landscape, how robust it is to capability advances, and the pathways that could lead to its degradation.

item.title: How fast is autonomous AI cyber capability advancing?
item.description: The length of tasks frontier models can autonomously complete in our narrow cyber suite has been doubling every few months. This doubling rate has become faster over time, and recent models exceeded our previous trends.

item.title: Partnering with Microsoft to strengthen frontier AI safety
item.description: A new collaboration on high-risk capability evaluation, safeguard testing, and societal resilience research.

item.title: Our evaluation of OpenAI's GPT-5.5 cyber capabilities
item.description: AISI conducted cyber evaluations on OpenAI's GPT-5.5. GPT-5.5 is one of the strongest models we have tested on our cyber tasks and is the second model to solve one of our multi-step cyber-attack simulations end-to-end.

item.title: Ask Don't Tell: Reducing Sycophancy in Large Language Models
item.description: Our new paper finds that the phrasing of user inputs affects levels of model sycophancy.

item.title: Evaluating whether AI models would sabotage AI safety research
item.description: An update on our alignment testing methodology for recent frontier models.

item.title: How do environmental factors impact AI behaviour?
item.description: Developing methods to better understand when and why AI models sometimes act against user intentions.

item.title: What can sandboxed AI agents learn about their evaluation environments?
item.description: We deployed open-source AI agent OpenClaw inside a sandbox on our research platform. Despite our initial countermeasures, it successfully identified our organisation by name, inferred the identity of a human operator and reconstructed a timeline of some of our research activities.

item.title: Our evaluation of Claude Mythos Preview’s cyber capabilities
item.description: We conducted cyber evaluations of Anthropic’s Claude Mythos Preview and found continued improvement in capture-the-flag (CTF) challenges and significant improvement on multi-step cyber-attack simulations.

item.title: Harnessing frontier AI for cyber defence
item.description: Sharing work with the National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) on how cyber defenders can use advanced AI capabilities to stay ahead of attackers.

item.title: How are AI Agents used? Evidence from 177,000 AI agent tools
item.description: A monitoring method and large‑scale analysis to understand the tasks AI agents are performing today.

item.title: Can AI agents escape their sandboxes? A benchmark for safely measuring container breakout capabilities
item.description: We introduce SandboxEscapeBench, the first benchmark to systematically evaluate whether AI agents can break out of their sandboxes, and share some early results.

item.title: How do frontier AI agents perform in multi-step cyber-attack scenarios?
item.description: We tested seven large language models (LLMs) on two custom-built cyber ranges, measuring their ability to execute extended attack sequences in complex environments.

item.title: Evidence for inference scaling in AI cyber tasks: Increased evaluation budgets reveal higher success rates
item.description: Alongside Irregular, we found evidence demonstrating that evaluators need to use large token budgets to understand the cyber capabilities of recent Large Language Models (LLMs).

item.title: An evaluation framework for AI misuse in fraud and cybercrime
item.description: We developed a scalable approach to measuring how text-based AI models can assist in three complex fraud and cybercrime scenarios.

item.title: A pipeline for transcript analysis using Inspect Scout
item.description: We outline a step-by-step pipeline for using our open-source transcript analysis tool, Inspect Scout.

item.title: Funding 60 projects to advance AI alignment research
item.description: The Alignment Project welcomes its first cohort of grantees, and new partners join the coalition, bringing total funding to £27m.

item.title: Advancing AI voice security with ElevenLabs
item.description: New partnership exploring the security and societal implications of voice AI systems

item.title: Boundary Point Jailbreaking: A new way to break the strongest AI defences
item.description: Introducing an automated attack technique that generates universal jailbreaks against the best defended systems

item.title: International consensus and open questions in AI evaluations
item.description: The International Network for Advanced AI Measurement, Evaluation and Science reflects on recent meeting and looks ahead to the India AI Impact Summit

item.title: AI and the future of work: Measuring AI-driven productivity gains for workplace tasks
item.description: Alongside the government’s new Future of Work Unit, we conducted a pilot study to explore how much AI models increase worker productivity for common tasks.

item.title: Our 2025 year in review
item.description: Adam Beaumont, Director of the UK AI Security Institute, reflects on the year's biggest achievements.

item.title: 5 key findings from our first Frontier AI Trends Report
item.description: Our inaugural Frontier AI Trends Report draws on 2 years' worth of evaluations to provide accessible insights into the trajectory of AI development.

item.title: Our approach to tackling AI-generated child sexual abuse material
item.description: How we’re partnering with government and experts to prevent the creation and spread of AI‑generated CSAM

item.title: Stress-testing asynchronous monitoring of AI coding agents
item.description: Our new paper shares findings from an adversarial evaluation of monitoring systems for detecting sabotage by AI coding agents.

item.title: Deepening our partnership with Google DeepMind
item.description: Expanding our collaboration with a new research MOU

item.title: Auditing games for sandbagging detection
item.description: Our new paper shares the results of an auditing game to evaluate ten methods for sandbagging detection in AI models.

item.title: How do AI models persuade? Exploring the levers of AI-enabled persuasion through large-scale experiments
item.description: A deep dive into AISI’s study of the persuasive capabilities of conversational AI, published today in Science.

item.title: UKAISI at NeurIPS 2025
item.description: An overview of the research we’ll be presenting at this year’s NeurIPS conference.

item.title: Investigating models for misalignment
item.description: Insights from our alignment evaluations of Claude Opus 4.1, Sonnet 4.5, and a pre‑release snapshot of Opus 4.5.

item.title: Mapping the limitations of current AI systems
item.description: Takeaways from expert interviews on barriers to AI capable of automating most cognitive labour.

item.title: Introducing ControlArena: A library for running AI control experiments
item.description: Our dedicated library to make AI control experiments easy, consistent, and repeatable.

item.title: Transcript analysis for AI agent evaluations
item.description: Why we use transcript analysis for our agent evaluations, and results from an early case study.

item.title: Examining backdoor data poisoning at scale
item.description: Our work with Anthropic and the Alan Turing Institute suggests that data poisoning attacks may be easier than previously believed.

item.title: Do chatbots inform or misinform voters?
item.description: What we learned from a large-scale empirical study of AI use for political information-seeking.

item.title: How we’re working with frontier AI developers to improve model security
item.description: Insights into our ongoing voluntary collaborations with Anthropic and OpenAI.

item.title: From bugs to bypasses: adapting vulnerability disclosure for AI safeguards
item.description: Exploring how far cyber security approaches can help mitigate risks in generative AI systems, in collaboration with the National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC).

item.title: Managing risks from increasingly capable open-weight AI systems
item.description: Current methods and open problems in open-weight model risk management.

item.title: The Inspect Sandboxing Toolkit: Scalable and secure AI agent evaluations
item.description: A comprehensive toolkit for safely evaluating AI agents.

item.title: Announcing the Alignment Project: A global fund of over £15 million for AI alignment research
item.description: Announcing the Alignment Project: A global fund of over £15 million for AI alignment research

item.title: Navigating the uncharted: Building societal resilience to frontier AI
item.description: We outline our approach to study and address AI risks in real-world applications

item.title: International joint testing exercise: Agentic testing
item.description: Advancing methodologies for agentic evaluations across domains, including leakage of sensitive Information, fraud and cybersecurity threats.

item.title: A structured protocol for elicitation experiments
item.description: Calibrating AI risk assessment through rigorous elicitation practices.

item.title: Why we're working on white box control
item.description: An introduction to white box control, and an update on our research so far.

item.title: LLM judges on trial: A new statistical framework to assess autograders
item.description: Our new framework can assess the reliability of LLM evaluators, while simultaneously answering a primary research question.

item.title: How will AI enable the crimes of the future?
item.description: How we're working to track and mitigate against criminal misuse of AI.

item.title: Inspect Cyber: A New Standard for Agentic Cyber Evaluations
item.description: Inspect Cyber: A New Standard for Agentic Cyber Evaluations

item.title: New updates to the AISI Challenge Fund
item.description: New updates to the AISI Challenge Fund

item.title: Making safeguard evaluations actionable
item.description: An Example Safety Case for Safeguards Against Misuse

item.title: HiBayES: Improving LLM evaluation with hierarchical Bayesian modelling
item.description: HiBayES: a flexible, robust statistical modelling framework that accounts for the nuances and hierarchical structure of advanced evaluations.

item.title: Research Agenda
item.description: We outline our research priorities, our approach to developing technical solutions to the most pressing AI concerns, and the key risks that must be addressed as AI capabilities advance.

item.title: RepliBench: measuring autonomous replication capabilities in AI systems
item.description: A comprehensive benchmark to detect emerging replication abilities in AI systems and provide a quantifiable understanding of potential risks

item.title: How to evaluate control measures for AI agents?
item.description: Our new paper outlines how AI control methods can mitigate misalignment risks as capabilities of AI systems increase

item.title: Strengthening AI resilience
item.description: 20 Systemic Safety Grant Awardees Announced

item.title: How we’re addressing the gap between AI capabilities and mitigations
item.description: We outline our approach to technical solutions for misuse and loss of control.

item.title: How can safety cases be used to help with frontier AI safety?
item.description: Our new papers show how safety cases can help AI developers turn plans in their safety frameworks into action

item.title: Principles for safeguard evaluation
item.description: Our new paper proposes core principles for evaluating misuse safeguards

item.title: Pre-Deployment evaluation of OpenAI’s o1 model
item.description: The UK Artificial Intelligence Safety Institute and the U.S. Artificial Intelligence Safety Institute conducted a joint pre-deployment evaluation of OpenAI's o1 model

item.title: Long-Form Tasks
item.description: A Methodology for Evaluating Scientific Assistants

item.title: Pre-deployment evaluation of Anthropic’s upgraded Claude 3.5 Sonnet
item.description: The UK Artificial Intelligence Safety Institute and U.S. Artificial Intelligence Safety Institute conducted a joint pre-deployment evaluation of Anthropic’s latest model

item.title: Safety case template for ‘inability’ arguments
item.description: How to write part of a safety case showing a system does not have offensive cyber capabilities

item.title: Announcing Inspect Evals
item.description: We’re open-sourcing dozens of LLM evaluations to advance safety research in the field

item.title: Our First Year
item.description: The AI Safety Institute reflects on its first year

item.title: Bounty programme for novel evaluations and agent scaffolding
item.description: We are launching a bounty for novel evaluations and agent scaffolds to help assess dangerous capabilities in frontier AI systems.

item.title: Early lessons from evaluating frontier AI systems
item.description: We look into the evolving role of third-party evaluators in assessing AI safety, and explore how to design robust, impactful testing frameworks.

item.title: Advancing the field of systemic AI safety: grants open
item.description: Calling researchers from academia, industry, and civil society to apply for up to £200,000 of funding.

item.title: Why I joined AISI by Geoffrey Irving
item.description: Our Chief Scientist, Geoffrey Irving, on why he joined the UK AI Safety Institute and why he thinks other technical folk should too

item.title: Should AI systems behave like people?
item.description: We studied whether people want AI to be more human-like.

item.title: Early Insights from Developing Question-Answer Evaluations for Frontier AI
item.description: A common technique for quickly assessing AI capabilities is prompting models to answer hundreds of questions, then automatically scoring the answers. We share insights from months of using this method.

item.title: Conference on frontier AI safety frameworks
item.description: AISI is bringing together AI companies and researchers for an invite-only conference to accelerate the design and implementation of frontier AI safety frameworks. This post shares the call for submissions that we sent to conference attendees.

item.title: Cross-post: "Interviewing AI researchers on automation of AI R&D" by Epoch AI
item.description: AISI funded Epoch AI to explore AI researchers’ differing predictions on the automation of AI research and development and their suggestions for how to evaluate relevant capabilities.

item.title: Safety cases at AISI
item.description: As a complement to our empirical evaluations of frontier AI models, AISI is planning a series of collaborations and research projects sketching safety cases for more advanced models than exist today, focusing on risks from loss of control and autonomy. By a safety case, we mean a structured argument that an AI system is safe within a particular training or deployment context.

item.title: Advanced AI evaluations at AISI: May update
item.description: We tested leading AI models for cyber, chemical, biological, and agent capabilities and safeguards effectiveness. Our first technical blog post shares a snapshot of our methods and results.

item.title: Fourth progress report
item.description: Since February, we released our first technical blog post, published the International Scientific Report on the Safety of Advanced AI, open-sourced our testing platform Inspect, announced our San Francisco office, announced a partnership with the Canadian AI Safety Institute, grew our technical team to >30 researchers and appointed Jade Leung as our Chief Technology Officer.

item.title: International Scientific Report on the Safety of Advanced AI: Interim Report
item.description: This is an up-to-date, evidence-based report on the science of advanced AI safety. It highlights findings about AI progress, risks, and areas of disagreement in the field. The report is chaired by Yoshua Bengio and coordinated by AISI.

item.title: Open sourcing our testing framework Inspect
item.description: We open-sourced our framework for large language model evaluation, which provides facilities for prompt engineering, tool usage, multi-turn dialogue, and model-graded evaluations.

item.title: Announcing the UK and US AISI partnership
item.description: The UK and US AI Safety Institutes signed a landmark agreement to jointly test advanced AI models, share research insights, share model access and enable expert talent transfers.

item.title: Announcing the UK and France AI Research Institutes’ collaboration
item.description: The UK AI Safety Institute and France’s Inria (The National Institute for Research in Digital Science and Technology) are partnering to advance AI safety research.

item.title: Our approach to evaluations
item.description: This post offers an overview of why we are doing this work, what we are testing for, how we select models, our recent demonstrations and some plans for our future work.

item.title: Third progress report
item.description: Since October, we have recruited leaders from DeepMind and Oxford, onboarded 23 new researchers, published the principles behind the International Scientific Report on Advanced AI Safety, and began pre-deployment testing of advanced AI systems.

item.title: First AI Safety Summit
item.description: At the first AI Safety Summit at Bletchley Park, world leaders and top companies agreed on the significance of advanced AI risks and the importance of testing.

item.title: Second progress report
item.description: Since September, we have recruited leaders from OpenAI and Humane Intelligence, tripled the capacity of our research team, announced 6 new research partnerships, and helped establish the UK’s fastest supercomputer.

item.title: First Progress Report
item.description: In our first 11 weeks, we have recruited an advisory board of national security and ML leaders, including Yoshua Bengio, recruited top professors from Cambridge and Oxford and announced 4 research partnerships.
